---
title: "Preliminaries on Computability and Algorithmic Information Theory"
date: 2026-03-03
abstract: "A concise introduction to computability and algorithmic information theory for mathematicians."
---

## Preliminaries

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Alphabet)"}
An alphabet $\Sigma$ is a finite non-empty set.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Words over an alphabet)"}
If $\Sigma$ is a finite set (alphabet), define $\Sigma^n$ to be the set of length-$n$ words over $\Sigma$ and
$$
\Sigma^*:=\bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty}\Sigma^n
$$
to be the set of all finite words over $\Sigma$.
We write $\epsilon$ for the unique word of length $0$ (the empty word), so $\Sigma^0=\{\epsilon\}$.
For $x\in \Sigma^*$ we write $|x|$ for its length, i.e. $x\in\Sigma^n\iff |x|=n$.
In particular, $\{0,1\}^*$ denotes the set of finite binary strings, and $|p|$ is the bit-length of $p\in\{0,1\}^*$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Languages)"}
A *language* over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is a subset $L\subseteq \Sigma^*$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Prefix and strict prefix)"}
<a id="def:prefix"></a>
Let $\Sigma$ be an alphabet and let $p,q\in\Sigma^*$.
We say that $p$ is a *prefix* of $q$ if there exists $r\in\Sigma^*$ such that $q=pr$.
If additionally $r\neq \epsilon$ (equivalently, $p\neq q$), then $p$ is a *strict prefix* of $q$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Prefix-free languages)"}
<a id="def:prefix-free-language"></a>
A language $L\subseteq \Sigma^*$ is *prefix-free* (a.k.a. *self-delimiting*) if there do not exist distinct $p,q\in L$ such that $p$ is a prefix of $q$.

*Example:* Over $\Sigma=\{0,1\}$, the language $L=\{0,01\}$ is not prefix-free because $0$ is a strict prefix of $01$.
In contrast, the set $T=\{0,10,110\}$ is prefix-free.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Partial functions)"}
Given sets $A,B$, a *partial function* $f:A\rightharpoonup B$ is a function $f:\mathrm{dom}(f)\to B$ with domain $\mathrm{dom}(f)\subseteq A$.
For $a\notin\mathrm{dom}(f)$ we say $f(a)$ is *undefined*.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Prefix-free functions)"}
<a id="def:prefix-free-functions"></a>
A partial function $f:\Sigma^*\rightharpoonup \Sigma^*$ is *prefix-free* (a.k.a. *self-delimiting*) if its domain
$$
\mathrm{dom}(f):=\{x\in\Sigma^*: f(x)\ \text{is defined}\}
$$
is a prefix-free language (in the sense of `def:prefix-free-language`).
:::

## Turing machines: the mechanics of computation

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (One-tape Turing machine)"}
<a id="def:tape-set"></a>
A deterministic one-tape Turing machine is a tuple $M=(Q,\Gamma,\sqcup,\Sigma,\delta,q_0,F)$ where

- $Q$ is a finite set of states,
- $\Sigma$ is an alphabet (the *input alphabet*), e.g. $\Sigma=\{0,1\}$,
- $\Gamma$ is an alphabet (the *tape alphabet*) with $\Sigma\cup\{\sqcup\}\subseteq\Gamma$, where $\sqcup\in\Gamma$ is the *distinguished blank symbol*,
- $q_0\in Q$ is a start state,
- $F\subseteq Q$ is a set of halting states, and
- $\delta:(Q\setminus F)\times \Gamma \to Q\times \Gamma \times \{L,R\}$ is the transition function, where $L$ (resp. $R$) means â€śmove the head one cell leftâ€ť (resp. right).

A *tape* over $\Gamma$ is a function $t:\mathbb{Z}\to \Gamma$ such that
$$
\mathrm{supp}_{\sqcup}(t):=\{i\in\mathbb{Z}: t(i)\neq \sqcup\}
$$
is a finite set (â€śall but finitely many tape cells are blankâ€ť).
Let $\mathcal{T}_\Gamma$ denote the set of all such tapes.

A *configuration* is a triple $(q,h,t)\in Q\times \mathbb{Z}\times \mathcal{T}_\Gamma$ where $q$ is the state, $t$ is the tape contents and $h$ is the head position.
If $q\in Q\setminus F$ and $\delta(q,t(h))=(q',a',d)$, define the next configuration to be $(q',h',t')$ where
$t'(h)=a'$, $t'(i)=t(i)$ for $i\neq h$, and $h'=h-1$ if $d=L$ while $h'=h+1$ if $d=R$.
So the transition function alters the tape at the previous location and then moves left or right. In particular the condition $t\in\mathcal{T}_\Gamma$ is preserved by the transition rule.

Given an input $x=x_0x_1\cdots x_{n-1}\in\Sigma^*$, define the initial configuration to be $(q_0,0,t_x)$ where
$t_x(i)=x_i$ for $i\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}$ and $t_x(i)=\sqcup$ otherwise.
Iterating the transition rule induces a discrete-time evolution of configurations.
We write $M(x)\downarrow$ if this evolution reaches some configuration with state in $F$ (halts) and $M(x)\uparrow$ otherwise (diverges).
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Acceptance, rejection, and decision)"}
Fix a Turing machine $M=(Q,\Gamma,\sqcup,\Sigma,\delta,q_0,F)$.
An *accepting set* is a subset $F_{\mathrm{acc}}\subseteq F$; the *rejecting set* is then $F_{\mathrm{rej}}:=F\setminus F_{\mathrm{acc}}$.
We say $M$ *accepts* an input $x$ if $M(x)\downarrow$ and the halting state lies in $F_{\mathrm{acc}}$, and $M$ *rejects* $x$ if $M(x)\downarrow$ and the halting state lies in $F_{\mathrm{rej}}$.

For a language $L\subseteq \Sigma^*$, we say $M$ *decides* $L$ if $M(x)\downarrow$ for all $x\in\Sigma^*$ and $M$ accepts $x$ iff $x\in L$ (equivalently, $M$ rejects $x$ iff $x\notin L$).

*Intuition:* deciding $L$ means membership in $L$ can be computed by an algorithm that always halts and returns a yes/no answer.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Remark (Why allow both left and right moves?)"}
Allowing the head to move both left and right is part of what makes the Turing machine model *universal*.
If one restricted to machines that only ever move right, the model never revisits earlier tape cells which prevents it from expressing all computable functions, e.g. it canâ€™t even decide whether a binary string is a palindrome.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Example (A simple one-tape Turing machine)"}
Let $\Sigma=\{0,1\}$ and $\Gamma=\{0,1,\sqcup\}$.
Define a Turing machine that computes the bitwise complement $x\mapsto \bar x$ (flip $0\leftrightarrow 1$) and halts:
let $Q=\{q_0,q_h\}$, $F=\{q_h\}$, and define $\delta$ on $Q\setminus F=\{q_0\}$ by
$$
\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,1,R),\qquad
\delta(q_0,1)=(q_0,0,R),\qquad
\delta(q_0,\sqcup)=(q_h,\sqcup,R).
$$
Starting at the leftmost input symbol, this machine scans right, flips each bit, and halts upon reaching the first blank.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Example (A palindrome-decider, informal)"}
Let $\mathrm{PAL}:=\{x\in\{0,1\}^*: x \text{ is a palindrome}\}$.
There is a one-tape Turing machine that decides $\mathrm{PAL}$ using the following routine.
Extend the tape alphabet to $\Gamma=\{0,1,\sqcup,X_0,X_1\}$, where $X_0$ (resp. $X_1$) marks a cell that originally contained $0$ (resp. $1$) and has already been matched.

Repeatedly: scan right from the left end to find the leftmost unmarked symbol $b\in\{0,1\}$ and replace it by $X_b$; then scan to the right end, move left to find the rightmost unmarked symbol (if none exists, accept), and compare it to $b$.
If it matches, replace it by $X_b$ and return to the left; if it mismatches, reject.

This halts and accepts exactly the palindromes because each iteration matches the current leftmost and rightmost unmatched symbols.
*Informal example:*
$$
01010\rightarrow X_01010\rightarrow X_0101X_0\rightarrow X_0X_101X_0\rightarrow X_0X_10X_1X_0\rightarrow X_0X_1X_0X_1X_0\rightarrow\operatorname{accept}.
$$
:::

## Computability: function computation

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Output convention)"}
Fix a one-tape Turing machine $M=(Q,\Gamma,\sqcup,\Sigma,\delta,q_0,F)$.
Write $\mathcal{T}_\Gamma$ for the set of tapes defined in `def:tape-set`, and define the set of *halting configurations* $\mathrm{Halt}(M):=F\times \mathbb{Z}\times \mathcal{T}_\Gamma$.
An *output convention* for $M$ is a function
$$
\mathrm{out}:\mathrm{Halt}(M)\to \Sigma^*.
$$

*Example:* two common choices are:

- *(Head-readout.)* If the halting tape is known (by construction of $M$) to contain only $\Sigma$-symbols on a contiguous block starting at the head, define $\mathrm{out}(q,h,t)$ to be that block.
- *(Origin-readout.)* If the halting tape is known to contain only $\Sigma$-symbols on cells $0,1,\dots,r$ and blanks elsewhere, define $\mathrm{out}(q,h,t):=t(0)t(1)\cdots t(r)$ where $r:=\max\{i\in\mathbb{Z}: t(i)\neq \sqcup\}$ (with $r=-1$ interpreted as output $\epsilon$).
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Computable and partial computable functions)"}
<a id="def:computable-partial-computable"></a>
Given a Turing machine $M$ with input alphabet $\Sigma$ and output convention $\mathrm{out}$, define the induced *partial output function* $\mathrm{out}_M:\Sigma^*\rightharpoonup \Sigma^*$ as follows:
for $x\in\Sigma^*$, if $M(x)\downarrow$ and the resulting halting configuration is $(q,h,t)\in \mathrm{Halt}(M)$, set
$$
\mathrm{out}_M(x):=\mathrm{out}(q,h,t),
$$
and if $M(x)\uparrow$ then leave $\mathrm{out}_M(x)$ undefined.

A (total) function $f:\Sigma^*\to\Sigma^*$ is *computable* if there exists a Turing machine $M$ with input alphabet $\Sigma$ such that $\mathrm{out}_M$ is total and $\mathrm{out}_M(x)=f(x)$ for all $x\in\Sigma^*$.

A partial function $f:\Sigma^*\rightharpoonup\Sigma^*$ is *partial computable* if there exists a Turing machine $M$ with input alphabet $\Sigma$ such that $\mathrm{out}_M=f$ as partial functions, i.e. $\mathrm{dom}(\mathrm{out}_M)=\mathrm{dom}(f)$ and whenever $x\in\mathrm{dom}(f)$ one has $\mathrm{out}_M(x)=f(x)$.
:::

::: {.callout-important title="Theorem (Robustness under computable post-processing)"}
If $f:\Sigma^*\rightharpoonup \Sigma^*$ is partial computable and $g:\Sigma^*\to \Sigma^*$ is total computable, then $g\circ f:\Sigma^*\rightharpoonup \Sigma^*$ is partial computable.
:::

This is a standard closure property of partial computable functions; see e.g. Cutland, *Computability: An Introduction to Recursive Function Theory* (1980), or Rogers, *Theory of Recursive Functions and Effective Computability* (1967/1987).

## Kolmogorov complexity

In this section we specialise to the *binary* alphabet $\{0,1\}^*$.^[This is standard and entails no loss of generality: any alphabet $\Sigma$ can be encoded into $\{0,1\}^*$ by a fixed computable injective map, changing Kolmogorov complexities by at most an additive $O(1)$ term.]
We work with two standard variants of Kolmogorov complexity:

- *plain* Kolmogorov complexity (denoted $C$), and
- *prefix-free* (a.k.a. *self-delimiting*) Kolmogorov complexity (denoted $K$).

::: {.callout-note title="Remark"}
Some ML papers use the symbol $K$ to denote the *plain* notion (what we call $C$); here we follow the standard Algorithmic Information Theory convention $C$ for plain and $K$ for prefix-free.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Plain Kolmogorov complexity relative to a machine)"}
Given a partial computable function $f:\{0,1\}^*\rightharpoonup \{0,1\}^*$, the *plain Kolmogorov complexity relative to $f$* is the function
$$
C_f:\{0,1\}^*\to \mathbb{N}\cup\{\infty\},\qquad
C_f(x)\;:=\;\min\{\,|p|: f(p)=x\,\}.
$$
*Example:* $C_f(x)=\infty$ if $x$ is not in the range of $f$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition (Prefix-free Kolmogorov complexity relative to a machine)"}
Given a prefix-free partial computable function $f$ (in the sense of `def:computable-partial-computable` and `def:prefix-free-functions`), its *prefix-free* (a.k.a. *self-delimiting*) Kolmogorov complexity is
$$
K_f:\{0,1\}^*\to \mathbb{N}\cup\{\infty\},\qquad
K_f(x)\;:=\;\min\{\,|p|: f(p)=x\,\}.
$$
:::
